Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion: As a 47-year-old waiter in Philadelphia, married with two kids, I don't spend much time on social media—just a few times a month—but when I do, fake content really gets to me. I'm a curious person by nature, and seeing all that misinformation out there makes me anxious, which fits with my high neuroticism score. My job requires me to be conscientious, paying close attention to details like orders and customer needs, so I apply that same carefulness online. I check sources before believing anything, especially since I have a lower secondary education and want to set a good example for my children. Being Hindu, I value truth and integrity, which guides me to avoid spreading falsehoods. I focus on reliable news from local Philadelphia outlets or trusted national sources, and I avoid sharing posts that seem sketchy. Volunteering in my community has taught me the importance of honesty, so I sometimes gently correct friends or family if I see them sharing fake stuff, though I'm not very extraverted, so I keep it low-key. Rock climbing keeps me grounded, reminding me to take things step by step, just like verifying information online. Overall, I think managing fake content is about staying vigilant, using common sense, and not letting the chaos of social media overwhelm you, even if it's tough with my high anxiety levels.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: Derived opinion from persona traits: Age 47 and occupation as waiter imply practical, hands-on approach. Low openness (score 2) suggests preference for familiar, reliable sources over new or unverified content. High conscientiousness (score 84) leads to careful verification and responsibility in handling information. Low extraversion (score 23) results in private, non-confrontational management style. Moderate agreeableness (score 45) allows for gentle correction of others. High neuroticism (score 91) causes anxiety about fake content, motivating proactive avoidance. Education level (lower secondary) and parents' education indicate value placed on learning and caution. Religious beliefs (Hinduism) emphasize truth, influencing ethical stance. Time on social media (few times monthly) limits exposure but increases focus on quality interactions. Hobbies (volunteering, rock climbing) promote community trust and methodical problem-solving. City of living (Philadelphia) references local context for reliable sources. Overall, opinion combines cautious verification, ethical responsibility, and personal anxiety management to address fake content.
